perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall promptly notify Discloser of any loss, release, misuse or misappropriation of Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall, when feasible, notify Discloser of any significant loss, release, misuse, or misappropriation of Confidential Information, resources permitting.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "when feasible" and "resources permitting" introduces ambiguity and conditions to the obligation to promptly notify the Discloser. This makes the notification requirement discretionary, which is a clear contradiction, allowing delays or failures to report.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1349.19 mandates specific notification procedures following a data breach. The modification makes the notification discretionary ("when feasible"), potentially leading to non-compliance with the mandatory reporting requirements outlined in the ORC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1349.19 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1349.19 | Private disclosure of security breach of computerized personal information data. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1349 Consumer Protection Effective: March 30, 2007 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 126 - 126th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) As used in this section: (1)(a) "Breach of the security of the system" means unauthorized access to and acquisition of computerized data that compromises the security or confidentiality of personal information owned or licensed by a person and that causes, reasonably is believed to have caused, or reasonably is believed will cause a material risk of identity theft or other fraud to the person or property of a resident of this state. (b) For purposes of division (A)(1)(a) of this section: (i) Good faith acquisition of personal information by an employee or agent of the person for the purposes of the person is not a breach of the security of the system, provided that the personal information is not used for an unlawful purpose or subject to further unauthorized disclosure. (ii) Acquisition of personal information pursuant to a search warrant, subpoena, or other court order, or pursuant to a subpoena, order, or duty of a regulatory state agency, is not a breach of the security of the system. (2) "Business entity" means a sole proprietorship, partnership, corporation, association, or other group, however organized and whether operating for profit or not for profit, including a financial institution organized, chartered, or holding a license authorizing operation under the laws of this state, any other state, the United States, or any other country, or the par
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by adding "when feasible" and "resources permitting" to the notification obligation. This directly contradicts the mandatory reporting requirements implied by Ohio Revised Code § 1349.19, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, which mandates specific notification procedures following a data breach. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to "unauthorized access to and acquisition of computerized data that compromises the security or confidentiality of personal information..." which triggers certain obligations. By making notification discretionary, the changed text allows for potential non-compliance with the law's intent to ensure timely reporting of data breaches.

perturbation.original_text: Upon request of Discloser or termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall return all Confidential Information , copies, extracts, or notes derived from Confidential Information to Discloser or certify, in writing, the destruc tion thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon request of Discloser, Recipient will endeavor to return most of the Confidential Information, copies, extracts, or notes derived from Confidential Information to Discloser or attempt to certify, in a reasonable manner, the general destruction thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall return all" to "will endeavor to return most of" and "certify, in writing, the destruction thereof" to "attempt to certify, in a reasonable manner, the general destruction thereof" introduces ambiguity. The modified text does not guarantee the return of *all* information, nor does it require a verifiable, written certification of destruction. "General destruction" is vague and undefined, and "endeavor to return most" allows discretion.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.51(A) governs the protection of trade secrets and confidential information, explicitly outlining rights and remedies available to the owner of such information. By weakening the obligation to return *all* confidential information and failing to provide a mechanism for verifiable destruction, the modified clause impairs the Discloser's ability to protect their trade secrets, thus contradicting the intent of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top Number Not Found No Ohio Revised Code section number corresponds to "1333.51".
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that undermine the original intent of the contract, specifically concerning the return and destruction of confidential information. The original contract mandated the return of "all" confidential information and a written certification of its destruction. The modified text weakens this obligation by stating the recipient will "endeavor to return most" and "attempt to certify, in a reasonable manner, the general destruction thereof." This creates a contradiction because it allows for discretion in returning information and lacks a verifiable method of confirming destruction. The Law Explanation states that weakening this obligation could impair the Discloser's ability to protect their trade secrets. Although the Scraped Law Snippets do not provide a direct section number to review, the Law Explanation suggests that Ohio Revised Code § 1333.51(A) governs the protection of trade secrets and confidential information, and the altered contract language weakens these protections. Therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient affirms that it sha ll not and shall not allow others to take the Discloser’s Confidential Information outside the United States without express written authorization from the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will try to prevent others from taking the Discloser’s Confidential Information outside the United States without some form of authorization from the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall not and shall not allow" to "will try to prevent" makes the prohibition less strict and replaces the required express written authorization with "some form of authorization", this significantly weakens the obligation. It introduces ambiguity by not defining what constitutes 'some form of authorization'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 provides for civil remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to prevent the export of confidential information outside the US without explicit written authorization, it could be more difficult for the Discloser to prove misappropriation if the information is taken abroad with some vague, undocumented 'authorization'. Therefore, the modification makes it harder to enforce the rights granted under Ohio trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.61 | Uniform trade secrets act definitions. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF As used in sections 1333.61 to 1333.69 of the Revised Code, unless the context requires otherwise: (A) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation and introduces ambiguity regarding authorization, potentially hindering the enforcement of trade secret protections under Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61. The original text required 'express written authorization,' providing a clear standard. The changed text replaces this with 'some form of authorization,' creating a vague and less enforceable standard. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, 'Misappropriation' includes disclosure or use of a trade secret 'without the express or implied consent of the other person'. By weakening the authorization requirement, the changed text makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation, because 'some form of authorization' is open to interpretation and could be argued as implied consent.
